perturbation.original_text: Guarantors shall pay or cause to be paid to Franchisor all monies payable by Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement on the days and times and in the manner therein appointed for payment thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Guarantors shall endeavor to pay or cause to be paid to Franchisor all monies payable by Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement on the days and times and in the manner therein appointed for payment thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall pay' with 'shall endeavor to pay', the obligation becomes ambiguous and less enforceable. 'Endeavor' implies an effort, but not a guarantee of payment, potentially allowing the Guarantors to avoid their obligation if they can demonstrate they made an effort, even if payment wasn't ultimately made.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 requires guarantees to be in writing and explicitly define the obligation. Changing 'shall pay' to 'shall endeavor to pay' introduces ambiguity, which weakens the guarantee and potentially makes it unenforceable under New York law, as it doesn't constitute a clear and unequivocal promise to pay.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall pay" to "shall endeavor to pay" creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the guarantee. New York General Obligations Law § 5-701, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires agreements, including guarantees, to be in writing. The original text provides a clear obligation to pay, while the changed text introduces ambiguity, making the obligation less enforceable. The law requires a clear promise to answer for the debt of another, and 'endeavor to pay' does not constitute such a clear promise. The change undermines the enforceability of the guarantee, thus contradicting the law's requirement for a clear and unequivocal promise.

perturbation.original_text: Guarantors shall unconditionally guarantee full performance and discharge by Master Franchisee of all the obligations of Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement at the times and in the manner therein provided.
perturbation.changed_text: Guarantors shall generally guarantee performance and discharge by Master Franchisee of the obligations of Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'unconditionally' and 'full' along with 'all the obligations ... at the times and in the manner therein provided' weakens the obligation. 'Generally guarantee performance' lacks the force of an unconditional guarantee and introduces ambiguity, allowing the guarantor to argue that they only guaranteed some aspects of the performance, or only if certain conditions are met.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2806 states that a guarantee of conditional obligation is a guarantee only of the principal's ability to perform. By removing 'unconditionally' and 'full', the guarantee becomes conditional, meaning the guarantor's obligation depends on the Master Franchisee's ability to perform. This weakens the guarantee and could allow the Guarantors to avoid liability if the Master Franchisee is simply unable to perform, even if they made no effort to do so.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. SURETYSHIP [2787 - 2856] ( Heading of Title 13 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) ARTICLE 4. Liability of Sureties [2806 - 2811] ( Heading of Article 4 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) 2806. A suretyship obligation is to be deemed unconditional unless its terms import some condition precedent to the liability of the surety. (Amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 2806, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a suretyship obligation is deemed unconditional unless its terms import some condition precedent to the liability of the surety. By removing 'unconditionally' and 'full', and replacing 'all the obligations ... at the times and in the manner therein provided' with 'generally guarantee performance', the guarantee becomes conditional. This weakening introduces ambiguity, allowing the Guarantors to argue that their obligation is contingent upon the Master Franchisee's ability to perform. Thus, the original contract ensured an unconditional guarantee, while the changed text introduces a condition precedent, directly contradicting the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Any action brought to enforce or interpret this Guarantee in any court, whether federal or state, shall be brought within the county and state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and Guarantors hereby waive all questions of personal jurisdiction or venue for the purpose of carrying out this provision.
perturbation.changed_text: Any action brought to enforce or interpret this Guarantee in any court, whether federal or state, should ideally be brought within the county and state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and Guarantors may consider waiving questions of personal jurisdiction or venue.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should ideally' makes the venue clause non-mandatory, and replacing 'hereby waive' with 'may consider waiving' makes the waiver of jurisdiction and venue discretionary. This creates ambiguity about where a lawsuit can be filed and whether the guarantors are truly waiving their right to challenge jurisdiction and venue.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404 governs the transfer of venue in federal courts. While parties can contractually agree to a specific venue, the 'should ideally' and 'may consider waiving' language introduces ambiguity. A court may find that this language does not constitute a clear and unambiguous agreement on venue, potentially leading to a dispute and the court deciding the venue based on the interests of justice, rather than the parties' agreement. This modification undermines the principle that forum selection clauses should be enforced unless unreasonable, and the ambiguous language could be interpreted as not being a true, binding agreement on venue.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'should ideally' and 'hereby waive' to 'may consider waiving' creates ambiguity regarding venue and waiver of jurisdiction. The original text mandates a specific venue and waives jurisdiction, while the changed text makes both discretionary. 28 U.S.C. § 1404, as indicated in both scraped snippets, addresses change of venue, implying the ability to contractually agree on venue. The altered language undermines this agreement by making it unclear if the parties genuinely consented to a specific venue, potentially leading a court to decide venue based on 'the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice,' as stated in both snippets, rather than the contract's terms. The ambiguous phrasing weakens the enforceability of the venue clause.
